text: The surgeon uses a suprapubic trans-fascial suture to hitch up the uterus, taking a bite onto the fundus to facilitate anterior exposure. The rectal growth is visible in the upper third peritonealized part of the rectum on the anterior wall.

The surgeon holds the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) in traction and incises the medial peritoneal fold at the base of the rectosigmoid to enter the plane of loose areolar tissue behind the IMA. The dissection proceeds in the TME plane, revealing the hypogastric nerves covered by fibro fatty tissue. The ureter is visible and pushed down during the medial to lateral dissection. The surgeon develops the TME plane for a short distance before transecting the IMA, ensuring the fibro fatty layer on the hypogastric nerves remains intact.

The surgeon focuses on the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), identifying the left colic artery arising from the IMA and traveling cephalad. The fibrofatty tissue surrounding the IMA is cleared, and lymph nodes at the IMA origin are included with the specimen. The IMA is skeletonized, freed from surrounding tissue, and doubly clipped before being divided.

The surgeon uses a gauze piece placed anterior to the ureter as a guide to divide the sigmoid lateral peritoneal fold, freeing the sigmoid colon. The peritoneal incision on the lateral peritoneal flap is extended cephalically, and the descending colon and sigmoid are mobilized from lateral to medial up to the splenic flexure. The ureter serves as a guide, and the white line of Toldt is visible, separating the colonic mesentery from the retroperitoneal fascia and Gerota's fascia. Blunt and sharp dissection along the white line of Toldt is used to mobilize the descending colon to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the lateral to medial mobilization of the splenic flexure by separating the omentum from the distal transverse colon. The gastrocolic and splenocolic ligaments are divided, followed by the linocolic ligament, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure mesentery from the underlying gerota fascia. The descending colon and splenic flexure are now completely mobilized.

The surgeon continues rectal mobilization along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, starting with posterior mobilization and moving posterolaterally before dividing lateral tissues. The left lateral peritoneal fold is divided, and the hypogastric nerves are visible at the seven o'clock position, covered by loose areolar tissue. The dissection remains close to the mesorectal fascia, preserving the loose areolar tissue over the presacral fascia and hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon joins the peritoneal incisions on either side in the cul-de-sac and proceeds with dissection into the rectovaginal space. The anterior mesorectum appears thin, while the posterior mesorectum is bulky. Dissection is performed carefully to keep the mesorectal fat on the anterior rectum and avoid injury to the posterior vaginal wall. The lateral ligaments, containing the middle rectal vessels, are divided using a harmonic device, staying close to the mesorectum to prevent nerve plexus injury.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the mesorectum five centimeters below the lower border of the upper rectal tumor, preparing the rectal wall for transection. The mesorectum is divided, with careful attention to avoid injury to the rectal wall, as it is thin anteriorly and bulky posteriorly. The anterolateral exposure of the rectal wall is completed, and the site is cleared for the application of the EndoGIS stapler.

The surgeon identifies a point of transection in the sigmoid colon approximately ten centimeters proximal to the tumor. The sigmoid mesentery is divided until reaching the colon wall, ensuring not to cut too close to the wall of the colon intended for anastomosis. The blood supply of the sigmoid and descending colon is maintained by the left colic artery and marginal artery, with potential reliance solely on the marginal artery if high ligation is performed.

The surgeon performs rectal transection using a blue or green stapler, depending on the rectal thickness. The rectum is pulled up in traction as the stapler is introduced through a port, ensuring a horizontal staple line. A second stapler firing aligns with the first to avoid dog ears. The rectal specimen is delivered through a left iliac fossa incision, and the anvil of a circular stapler is placed. After anal dilatation, the circular stapler is introduced into the rectal stump, and an end-to-side anastomosis is completed without twisting or tension. The anastomotic integrity is tested with methylene blue, and a pelvic drain is placed.
